encounters.label: encounters — one row per hospital stay
encounters.setup:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "ward":      ["ICU", "ICU", "Cardio", "Cardio", "Cardio", "Neuro"],
    "age":       [71, 64, None, 58, 81, 49],
    "los_days":  [3, 9, 2, 5, 40, 4],
    "admit_date": ["2026-01-03", "2026-01-07", "2026-02-11",
                   "2026-02-12", "2026-03-01", "2026-03-04"],
    "diagnosis": ["sepsis", "Sepsis, severe", "angina",
                  "MI", "sepsis", "stroke"],
})

revenue.label: revenue — quarterly revenue by region

wide_measures.label: wide_measures — one row per patient, measures in columns

tasks.question: Which method returns the label of the maximum value in a Series?
tasks.why: max returns the value itself; idxmax returns the index label of that value; argmax returns the integer position.

tasks.question: A length-of-stay column has a few very long outlier stays. Which statistic best describes a typical stay?
tasks.why: The median is robust to outliers; the mean is pulled toward the long tail and overstates the typical stay.

tasks.question: Within a groupby, what is the difference between size() and count()?
options: size() includes NaN rows; count() excludes them
count() includes NaN rows; size() excludes them
size() works only on numeric columns
tasks.why: size() counts all rows in each group including missing values; count() counts only non-null values per column.

tasks.question: df.iloc[0] selects a row by what?
tasks.why: iloc is positional (0 = first row regardless of the index); loc is label-based.

tasks.question: By default, df.dropna() removes a row if…
options: all of its values are missing
any one of its values is missing
tasks.why: The default is how='any' — a single NaN anywhere in the row drops it. Use how='all' or subset= to narrow this.

tasks.question: You want every row of the left table kept, with matching columns added from the right where a key exists. Which join?
tasks.why: A left join keeps all left rows; unmatched right columns become NaN. inner would drop unmatched left rows.

tasks.question: A column of ages loaded as text contains the value "unknown". Which is the safest conversion?
tasks.why: astype(int) raises on "unknown"; to_numeric with errors='coerce' turns unconvertible values into NaN so the rest survives.

tasks.question: To create a column that is "high" when value > 100 else "low", the idiomatic tool is…
tasks.why: np.where is a vectorised if-else over the whole column — far faster and clearer than row-wise loops.

tasks.question: Which gives the proportion of each category rather than the raw count?
tasks.why: normalize=True divides each count by the total, yielding proportions that sum to 1.

tasks.question: For a monthly grouping key that sorts correctly across years, prefer…
tasks.why: dt.month gives 1-12 with no year, so 2024-01 and 2025-01 collide; to_period('M') keeps year+month and sorts chronologically.

tasks.question: Compute the median length of stay per ward as a Series.
tasks.hint: Combine groupby with a column selection and median().
tasks.why: Group-then-stat is the canonical split-apply-combine pattern; median is robust to outlier stays.

tasks.question: Keep only rows where the age column is not missing.
tasks.why: subset restricts the missing-value test to one column instead of dropping a row for any NaN anywhere.

tasks.question: Select all rows where age is at least 65.
tasks.hint: Build a boolean mask and index with it.
tasks.why: A boolean Series passed to df[...] keeps the rows where it is True — the standard filtering idiom.

tasks.question: Return the 3 rows with the highest revenue.
tasks.hint: There is a dedicated method; no full sort needed.
tasks.why: nlargest is clearer and cheaper than sort_values(...).head(3) when you only need the top N.

tasks.question: Convert the text column admit_date into real datetimes, in place.
tasks.hint: pd.to_datetime, assigned back to the column.
tasks.why: Dates arrive as strings; parsing to datetime64 unlocks the .dt accessor, sorting, and time arithmetic.

tasks.question: Build a table of mean revenue with region as rows and quarter as columns.
tasks.hint: pivot_table with index, columns, values, aggfunc.
tasks.why: pivot_table produces a wide, display-ready summary and aggregates duplicates automatically.

tasks.question: Select rows where diagnosis contains the word "sepsis" (case-insensitive).
tasks.hint: Use the .str accessor; pass case and na arguments.
tasks.why: na=False prevents NaN cells from raising when the mask indexes rows; case=False makes the match case-insensitive.

tasks.question: Reshape the wide table (id column patient) into long format.
tasks.why: melt turns named measure columns into variable/value rows — the tidy layout grouping and plotting expect.

tasks.question: Create a reproducible random generator seeded with 42.
tasks.hint: The modern NumPy API is np.random.default_rng.
tasks.why: A seeded default_rng makes random results reproducible; the legacy np.random.seed global state is discouraged.

tasks.question: What is the shape of the result?
options: One row per ward (a small Series)
Same length as the original df (a Series of length n_rows)
tasks.why: transform broadcasts the per-group statistic back to every original row, so the result aligns 1:1 with df.

tasks.question: What happens?
options: The flag column is set to 1 for those rows
A SettingWithCopyWarning; the assignment may not stick
tasks.why: Chained indexing assigns into a temporary copy. Use df.loc[df['age'] > 65, 'flag'] = 1 instead.

tasks.code:
import numpy as np
np.nan == np.nan
tasks.question: What does this evaluate to?
tasks.why: NaN is not equal to anything, including itself. Test for missingness with isna()/isnull(), never == np.nan.

tasks.code:
left["id"]  # dtype int64
right["id"] # dtype object ('001','002')
left.merge(right, on="id")
tasks.question: What is the likely outcome?
options: Zero matched rows — keys differ by dtype
Pandas auto-casts and it just works
tasks.why: Integer 1 never equals string '001'. Align key dtypes before merging or the join silently produces no matches.

tasks.question: What is the dtype of s?
tasks.why: A classic int column with a missing value is upcast to float64, because NaN is a float. Use the nullable Int64 dtype to keep integers.
